Emergency First-Aid Guide
Select an Emergency
Language
English
ಕನ್ನಡ (Kannada)
Snake Bite
Heart Attack
Fracture
Choking
Burns
Allergy
Stroke
Poisoning
Bleeding
Heat Stroke
Drowning
Electric Shock
Fainting
Dog Bite
Asthma Attack
Seizures
Head Injury
Nosebleed
Insect Sting
Diabetic Emergency
Next
Previous

Nearby Hospitals (Offline)
Share Info
Keep the person calm. Move them away from the snake.
Remove jewelry before swelling starts.
Keep the bitten area at or below heart level.
Clean the wound. Do NOT use water or a tourniquet.
Call an ambulance immediately. Help them sit or lie down.
Loosen any tight clothing.
Give 300mg Aspirin if they are not allergic.
Be prepared to perform CPR if they pass out.
Stop bleeding with a clean cloth.
Immobilize the area. Do NOT realign the bone.
Apply ice packs to limit swelling.
Treat for shock. Lay them flat with feet elevated.
Stand behind them and wrap arms around their waist.
Place a fist just above the navel.
Perform 5 quick, upward abdominal thrusts.
Repeat until the object is forced out.
Cool the burn with cool running water for 10-20 mins.
Remove rings before the area swells.
Cover loosely with a sterile gauze bandage.
Take a pain reliever like ibuprofen if needed.
Identify and remove the allergen.
Help them use an EpiPen immediately if they have one.
Have them lie still on their back with feet raised.
Seek medical help even if symptoms improve.
Note the time when symptoms started.
Check for face drooping on one side.
Check if one arm drifts downward when raised.
Check for slurred speech. Call an ambulance.
Find out what was swallowed and how much.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless told by a doctor.
If conscious, rinse mouth with water.
Call poison control or emergency services.
Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth.
Maintain pressure until bleeding stops.
Add more cloth if soaked. Do not remove the first.
Elevate the injury above the heart if possible.
Move them to a cool, shaded area.
Remove excess clothing and spray with cool water.
Fan them to lower body temperature.
If conscious, give cool water to sip slowly.
Get help. Call emergency services.
Remove them from water safely.
Check breathing. If not breathing, start CPR.
If breathing, keep them warm in recovery position.
Do NOT touch them if still in contact with power.
Turn off power or use a dry wooden object to move them.
Call an ambulance and check breathing.
Start CPR if needed. Cover burns with dry cloth.
Lay them on their back. Elevate feet 12 inches.
Loosen tight clothing (belts, collars).
Check if they are breathing.
If they don\'t wake in a minute, call medical help.
Wash wound with mild soap and water.
Apply pressure to stop any bleeding.
Apply a sterile bandage.
See a doctor for rabies and infection check.
Help them sit upright and stay calm.
Loosen tight clothing to aid breathing.
Help them use their quick-relief inhaler.
Call for help if symptoms worsen.
Move objects away. Do NOT restrain them.
Place something soft under their head.
Time the seizure. Note its duration.
After it ends, roll them onto their side.
Keep them still. Minimize head/neck movement.
Apply firm pressure to any bleeding.
Monitor for confusion or vomiting.
Seek medical help for severe trauma.
Sit upright and lean forward. Do NOT lean back.
Pinch the soft part of the nose for 10-15 mins.
Breathe through mouth. Apply ice pack to nose.
If bleeding lasts over 20 mins, see a doctor.
Remove stinger with a flat edge (like a card).
Wash the area with soap and water.
Apply a cold pack to reduce swelling.
Monitor for severe allergic reactions.
If conscious, give sugar (juice, candy).
If unconscious, do NOT give food. Call help.
Have them rest until they feel better.
Seek medical help if symptoms persist.
City Emergency Hospital
Distance: 1.2 km
Rural Life Care Center
Distance: 3.5 km
St. Mary First Aid Clinic
Distance: 5.0 km
